persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: the dossier flags the bio as single-initial ('R'), so the namesake trap is formally in play — a second R. Stout (a son, say) could be imagined on the New Zealand bench. This fails on every dimension: the staff lists print the full forename ('Sir Robert Stout'), not just an initial, for most years; the office is the unique Chief Justiceship of New Zealand, which the biography gives as 'chief justice of N.Z., 1899-1926' while the stint runs 1900-1922 wholly inside that span; every stint row carries K.C.M.G., matching the bio's K.C.M.G. (1886); and bio and stint co-occur in 15 edition-years at position similarity 100. A rival Robert Stout who was also a K.C.M.G. knight and also Chief Justice during 1900-1922 cannot exist alongside the bio's man, since the office has one holder. The 'honoured retiree' trap does not apply: the bio's tenure (to 1926) outlasts the stint's final listing (1922), so the listings reflect current service.

evidence_cited.fact: Sir Robert Stout | Chief Justice | Judicial | K.C.M.G.

persons.would_overturn: Evidence that the New Zealand Chief Justiceship passed to a different Robert Stout within 1900-1922, or that the bio's 1899-1926 tenure is a conflation of two judges — neither has any support in the dossier.

notes: Despite the single-initial warning on the bio record, the staff lists themselves print the full forename 'Robert', and the match rests on a unique office (Chief Justice of New Zealand) with the stint span 1900-1922 nested inside the bio's printed tenure 1899-1926, a shared dated honour (K.C.M.G. 1886, present in every row), and 15 co-occurring edition-years. All deterministic checks pass; nothing in the dossier supports a counter-hypothesis.
